prompt: BEAP (Building Energy Assessment Professional) certification emphasizes:

options.text: On-site assessment of existing building energy performance and improvement recommendations
Only new-construction energy modeling without field visits
Structural peer review of high-rise foundations
BACnet controller programming certification exclusively

explanation: BEAP validates skills in evaluating how buildings use energy and identifying practical improvements.

distractorExplanations.b: BEAP focuses on assessing existing buildings in the field.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural engineering is outside BEAP scope.
distractorExplanations.d: Controls knowledge supports assessment but is not the sole focus.

prompt: ASHRAE Level I energy audit (walk-through) typically includes:

options.text: Preliminary energy-use analysis, walk-through survey, and low-cost/no-cost recommendations
Submetering every end use with year-long M&V contracts only
No review of utility bills or operating practices
Complete HVAC redesign construction documents

explanation: Level I identifies obvious opportunities without the capital cost detail of higher audit levels.

distractorExplanations.b: Detailed submetering is characteristic of Level III.
distractorExplanations.c: Utility analysis is fundamental even at Level I.
distractorExplanations.d: Level I is screening-level, not design-ready.

prompt: A Level II energy audit adds to Level I by providing:

options.text: Energy survey and analysis with sufficient detail to justify implementation of identified ECMs
Only a one-page letter with no equipment inventory
Guaranteed contractor pricing for all trades nationally
Elimination of any financial analysis

explanation: Level II bridges screening and investment-grade analysis with measure-level savings estimates.

distractorExplanations.b: Level II requires more detailed survey and analysis.
distractorExplanations.c: Level II supports budgeting; it does not replace bid procurement.
distractorExplanations.d: Level II includes economic screening of measures.

prompt: Level III investment-grade audits are justified when:

options.text: Large capital decisions require high-confidence savings, risk allocation, and detailed M&V planning
A tenant requests a single LED lamp replacement
No utility data exists and none can be obtained
The owner wants only a roof warranty review

explanation: Financing, ESPCs, and major retrofits often require Level III rigor.

distractorExplanations.b: Small measures rarely need investment-grade analysis.
distractorExplanations.c: Audits require energy consumption data.
distractorExplanations.d: Roof warranties alone do not define Level III scope.

prompt: Energy Use Intensity (EUI) is calculated as:

options.text: Annual site energy consumption divided by gross floor area
Peak demand divided by occupant count only
Water use in gallons divided by cooling tons
Source energy multiplied by floor area

explanation: EUI enables benchmarking across buildings of different sizes.

distractorExplanations.b: EUI normalizes total energy by area, not peak per person.
distractorExplanations.c: EUI addresses site energy, not water metrics.
distractorExplanations.d: EUI divides energy by area; it does not multiply.

prompt: Portfolio benchmarking with ENERGY STAR scores compares a building to:

options.text: National survey data (e.g., CBECS) for similar building type and climate
Only the architect's design intent document
Structural steel tonnage in comparable towers
Refrigerant GWP values exclusively

explanation: Normalized scores reflect percentile performance vs peers, not absolute kWh alone.

distractorExplanations.b: Scores use operational data vs national peer groups.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural weight is unrelated to ENERGY STAR scoring.
distractorExplanations.d: ENERGY STAR scoring is based on energy performance metrics.

prompt: During a walk-through assessment, a BEAP should document:

options.text: Equipment nameplates, control settings, maintenance condition, and obvious operational anomalies
Only lobby paint colors for branding review
Personal opinions without photos or notes
Nothing, relying solely on owner memory

explanation: Thorough notes and photos create an audit trail for measure development.

distractorExplanations.b: Decor finishes do not substitute for equipment documentation.
distractorExplanations.c: Defensible assessments require recorded observations.
distractorExplanations.d: Field documentation supports recommendations and M&V.

prompt: Utility bill analysis often begins by:

options.text: Normalizing consumption for weather, occupancy, and rate changes before trend comparison
Ignoring all demand charges in every tariff
Comparing raw kWh only with no time period alignment
Using only the most recent week of data always

explanation: Degree-day normalization and regression separate weather effects from operational changes.

distractorExplanations.b: Demand charges can be a major cost driver.
distractorExplanations.c: Normalization reveals true performance changes.
distractorExplanations.d: Annual or multi-year trends require longer periods.

prompt: A common BEAP finding in constant-volume HVAC systems is:

options.text: Simultaneous heating and cooling or reheat at part load causing excess energy use
Zero ventilation because outdoor air is disabled by code
Perfect efficiency with no improvement potential
No relationship between airflow and thermostat calls

explanation: Older CV/reheat and poor scheduling are frequent existing-building issues.

distractorExplanations.b: Codes require minimum ventilation; misuse causes energy waste differently.
distractorExplanations.c: Constant-volume systems often have significant retrofit opportunities.
distractorExplanations.d: Poor zone control is a common assessment finding.

prompt: The BEAP exam passing score published by ASHRAE is approximately:

options.text: 69 correct out of 100 scored items
68 correct out of 100 scored items (verify current FAQs before scheduling)
83 correct out of 120 scored items
45 correct out of 60 scored items

explanation: Each ASHRAE credential has a distinct Angoff-derived pass point.

distractorExplanations.a: 69/100 is the BEMP pass point, not BEAP.
distractorExplanations.c: 83/120 applies to BCxP.
distractorExplanations.d: BEAP uses a 100-scored-item form, not 60.

prompt: When prioritizing ECMs, a BEAP typically considers:

options.text: Savings potential, implementation cost, disruption, and owner constraints
Only measures with the longest payback regardless of owner goals
Alphabetical order of equipment names
Measures that require no O&M follow-up ever

explanation: Screening matrices rank measures by cost, savings, and feasibility.

distractorExplanations.b: Prioritization balances economics with owner objectives.
distractorExplanations.c: Technical and economic merit drive prioritization.
distractorExplanations.d: All measures need some operational consideration.

prompt: Lighting assessment in an existing building should capture:

options.text: Fixture types, lamp/ballast technology, controls, hours of operation, and maintained illuminance needs
Only exterior landscaping irrigation schedules
Wattage guesses without fixture counts
Ceiling tile brand exclusively

explanation: Accurate lighting inventories underpin LED retrofit and control ECMs.

distractorExplanations.b: Irrigation is unrelated to lighting assessment.
distractorExplanations.c: Inventory and connected load data support savings calculations.
distractorExplanations.d: Ceiling products do not define lighting power or control strategy.

prompt: Envelope assessment for energy performance includes checking for:

options.text: Air leakage paths, insulation continuity, window condition, and shading effectiveness
Only interior furniture layout
Elevator counterweight alloy grade
Kitchen menu planning

explanation: Infiltration, conduction, and solar gain drive heating and cooling loads.

distractorExplanations.b: Furniture may affect loads but envelope assessment focuses on the shell.
distractorExplanations.c: Elevator materials are unrelated to envelope thermal performance.
distractorExplanations.d: Food service menus are not envelope assessment items.

prompt: Boiler plant assessment should verify:

options.text: Combustion efficiency, staging, setpoints, distribution losses, and maintenance history
Only the paint color of the boiler room door
That boilers never require combustion air
Absence of any safety controls

explanation: Plant efficiency testing and turndown capability reveal major gas savings opportunities.

distractorExplanations.b: Cosmetic items do not indicate combustion performance.
distractorExplanations.c: Boilers require proper combustion air per code and manufacturer data.
distractorExplanations.d: Safety controls are mandatory and affect reliable operation.

prompt: Compressed air leaks identified during assessment:

options.text: Increase motor-driven load and should be quantified as a potential quick-payback ECM
Have no effect on electricity because air is free
Only matter in residential kitchens
Cannot be detected without demolition

explanation: Leak repair and pressure optimization are classic low-cost industrial ECMs.

distractorExplanations.b: Compressors consume significant electricity to maintain pressure.
distractorExplanations.c: Compressed air systems are common in commercial/industrial facilities.
distractorExplanations.d: Leaks are often found audibly and with simple detection tools.

prompt: A BEAP reviewing BAS trend data should look for:

options.text: Equipment short-cycling, valves stuck open, economizers disabled, and setpoint overrides
Only the wallpaper pattern in the control room
Guaranteed absence of any alarms
Structural column sizes in drawings

explanation: Operational anomalies visible in trends frequently explain poor EUI vs peers.

distractorExplanations.b: Trend review is a technical operations diagnostic.
distractorExplanations.c: Alarms and overrides often reveal waste.
distractorExplanations.d: Structural design is separate from BAS trend analysis.

prompt: Domestic hot water assessment includes:

options.text: Storage temperatures, recirculation control, insulation, and peak demand characteristics
Only cold water fountain aesthetics
Refrigerant charge in rooftop units only
Parking lot striping layout

explanation: Oversized storage, high setpoints, and uncontrolled recirculation waste energy.

distractorExplanations.b: DHW systems require temperature and distribution review.
distractorExplanations.c: RTU refrigerant is unrelated to DHW assessment.
distractorExplanations.d: Site striping does not define DHW energy use.

prompt: When gas and electric meters serve mixed loads, a BEAP should:

options.text: Allocate consumption to HVAC, DHW, and process uses using submeters, engineering estimates, or short-term monitoring
Assume 100% of all fuels are HVAC without analysis
Discard gas data if any electric load exists
Ignore process loads in industrial facilities

explanation: Allocation prevents overstating HVAC retrofit savings.

distractorExplanations.b: Mixed-use meters require allocation for accurate ECM savings.
distractorExplanations.c: Both fuels may be relevant to the assessment.
distractorExplanations.d: Process loads often dominate and must be separated for HVAC ECMs.

prompt: A screening-level simple payback calculation divides:

options.text: Implementation cost by annual energy cost savings
Annual savings by implementation cost incorrectly for payback years
Peak kW by floor area
Occupant count by ventilation rate

explanation: Simple payback is a common Level II economic screening tool.

distractorExplanations.b: Payback years equal cost divided by annual savings.
distractorExplanations.c: That ratio is not simple payback.
distractorExplanations.d: Ventilation metrics do not define financial payback.

prompt: ASHRAE audit procedures reference collecting:

options.text: Architectural, mechanical, electrical, and control drawings plus O&M manuals when available
Only social media posts about the building
No historical data to speed the site visit
Only future tenant lease marketing brochures

explanation: Document review precedes and supports field verification.

distractorExplanations.b: Technical documents, not social media, support audits.
distractorExplanations.c: Background data improves assessment quality.
distractorExplanations.d: Marketing materials rarely contain operating data.

prompt: Night setback or setup strategies assessed on site should be checked against:

options.text: Actual BAS schedules and after-hours occupancy needs
Only peak summer cooling tons from the original design
The building's street address numerology
Mandatory 24/7 full conditioning in all buildings

explanation: Schedule optimization is a low-cost ECM when operations allow.

distractorExplanations.b: Schedules are operational parameters distinct from design capacity.
distractorExplanations.c: Address has no bearing on HVAC scheduling.
distractorExplanations.d: Many buildings can safely setback during unoccupied hours.

prompt: Steam trap failure identified during assessment typically causes:

options.text: Live steam loss, excess boiler load, and higher water treatment costs
Improved heating efficiency with no fuel impact
Reduced need for any boiler maintenance
Lower water consumption always

explanation: Trap surveys are a standard industrial and campus steam ECM.

distractorExplanations.b: Failed traps waste steam and fuel.
distractorExplanations.c: Trap failures increase system stress and maintenance needs.
distractorExplanations.d: Lost steam increases makeup water demand.

prompt: Cooling tower assessment should note:

options.text: Approach temperature, fan control, drift eliminators, and basin heating/bleed operation
Only the color of the tower fill without performance data
That towers never affect chiller kW
Structural footing depth exclusively

explanation: Poor tower maintenance raises condenser temperatures and chiller energy.

distractorExplanations.b: Operating conditions determine chiller plant efficiency.
distractorExplanations.c: Tower performance directly affects condenser water temperature and chiller efficiency.
distractorExplanations.d: Footings do not define tower thermal performance.

prompt: A BEAP recommendation report should clearly separate:

options.text: Operational changes, capital ECMs, and further study needs with estimated savings ranges
All items into one paragraph with no costs
Only measures the assessor personally prefers aesthetically
Confidential findings with no supporting data

explanation: Structured reports help owners fund and implement improvements.

distractorExplanations.b: Clients need categorized measures with economic context.
distractorExplanations.c: Recommendations must be evidence-based and economic.
distractorExplanations.d: Reports should be transparent and actionable.

prompt: Infrared thermography during assessment can help identify:

options.text: Missing insulation, air leakage, and overloaded electrical connections indicative of waste or safety issues
Only interior paint sheen preferences
Guaranteed annual utility rates
Occupant job titles

explanation: IR surveys support envelope and electrical distribution assessments.

distractorExplanations.b: Thermography reveals thermal anomalies, not finish schedules.
distractorExplanations.c: Tariffs come from utilities, not IR cameras.
distractorExplanations.d: Thermography is a physical diagnostic tool.
